name: Law of Demeter

forces: Coupling to a collaborator’s internal structure ↔ convenience of reaching through it
Stability of interfaces ↔ deep object graphs
x.structure: A method should only call methods on: itself, its parameters, objects it creates, and its direct fields — not objects returned by those (no a.getB().getC().doX()).
antiPatterns: Feature envy reaching into another object’s internals
x.scale: Method / class level; an object-design heuristic, not an architecture rule.

def: A coupling guideline: an object should talk only to its immediate collaborators, not reach through them into their internals.
problem: Chained navigation (a.getB().getC()...) couples callers to the internal structure of unrelated objects, so distant changes ripple widely.
mechanism: Restrict each method’s calls to "close" objects (self, parameters, created objects, direct fields). Where you would reach through, add a delegating method to the collaborator instead ("tell, don’t ask").
limits: Can spawn many delegating wrapper methods
Misapplied to data/DTOs and fluent builders
A heuristic, not a hard law
failures: "Law" treated literally, wrapping trivial data access
Fluent / builder APIs flagged as violations
myths: "Any method chain violates Demeter" — chains on the same fluent object or on data are fine
